event: Early hominins inhabit the Eritrean region, with the Buya skull (one of the oldest near-complete hominid crania, dated to roughly one million years ago) later found in the Danakil Depression.
Stone tools and obsidian artifacts at coastal sites like Abdur on the Red Sea show some of the earliest evidence of humans exploiting marine resources.
Neolithic communities along the highlands and coast develop herding, foraging, and early settlement patterns.
The Eritrean coast forms part of the Land of Punt, a trading partner sought by ancient Egypt for gold, incense, ebony, and exotic goods.
The Pre-Aksumite Gash Group and D'mt-era polities flourish, linking the Eritrean highlands with South Arabian and Sabaean cultural influences.
Settlements such as Qohaito and Metera grow as part of the D'mt kingdom's sphere, with monumental stone architecture and inscriptions.
The port of Adulis on the Red Sea becomes a major emporium of the Aksumite trade network, connecting the interior to Rome, India, and Arabia.
The Kingdom of Aksum rises to dominate the region, with Adulis serving as its principal seaport for ivory, gold, and incense exports.
King Ezana of Aksum converts to Christianity, spreading the faith through the highlands of present-day Eritrea and Ethiopia.
Aksum launches a military campaign across the Red Sea from Adulis to conquer Himyarite Yemen.
Early Muslim refugees fleeing persecution in Mecca cross the Red Sea, and Islam begins to spread along the Eritrean coast and to the Dahlak Islands.
Aksum's power declines as trade routes shift and Adulis fades, while the Dahlak Archipelago emerges as a Muslim trading sultanate.
The Zagwe dynasty rises in the highlands, maintaining Christian rule over parts of the Eritrean plateau.
The Solomonic dynasty is restored in Abyssinia, with the northern province of Medri Bahri (later Eritrean highlands) under its influence.
The Ottoman Empire begins extending control over the Red Sea coast, seizing the port of Massawa and the Dahlak Islands.
The Ottomans formally establish the province of Habesh, governing Massawa and the Eritrean coastal lowlands.
Egyptian and later Ottoman-Egyptian authorities tighten control over Massawa and the coastal trade, including the slave trade through Red Sea ports.
Egypt under Khedive Ismail takes over Massawa from the Ottomans and pushes inland toward the Eritrean highlands.
An Italian shipping company purchases the port of Assab on the Red Sea, marking the first Italian foothold in the region.
The Italian government takes over Assab, formally beginning Italian colonial expansion on the Eritrean coast.
Italian forces occupy Massawa, extending control over the coast during the Scramble for Africa.
Ethiopian forces defeat the Italians at the Battle of Dogali, briefly halting Italian inland expansion.
Italy formally proclaims the colony of Eritrea, naming it after the Latin term for the Red Sea and making Asmara its eventual capital.
Ethiopia crushes Italy at the Battle of Adwa, confining Italian ambitions and confirming Eritrea's borders as an Italian colony.
Italy uses Eritrea as the staging ground for its conquest of Ethiopia, merging the territories into Italian East Africa.
British forces defeat the Italians at the Battle of Keren and take control of Eritrea, beginning a period of British military administration.
The United Nations resolves to federate Eritrea with Ethiopia under the Ethiopian crown while granting Eritrea autonomy.
Eritrea is formally federated with Ethiopia, with its own parliament, flag, and constitution under the UN arrangement.
Eritrean activists found the Eritrean Liberation Movement, an early underground organization opposing Ethiopian domination.
The Eritrean Liberation Front launches the armed struggle for independence, beginning a thirty-year war.
Emperor Haile Selassie dissolves the Eritrean federation and annexes Eritrea as an Ethiopian province, intensifying the rebellion.
The Eritrean People's Liberation Front emerges as a Marxist-oriented breakaway that becomes the dominant independence movement.
Ethiopia's emperor is overthrown by the Derg military junta, which escalates the war against Eritrean fighters.
Eritrean rebels capture most of the territory before Soviet-backed Ethiopian forces launch massive counter-offensives.
The EPLF wins a decisive victory at the Battle of Afabet, shattering Ethiopian army positions in northern Eritrea.
EPLF forces capture Asmara as the Derg regime collapses, and a provisional Eritrean government is established.
Eritreans vote overwhelmingly for independence in a UN-monitored referendum, and Eritrea is declared a sovereign state and admitted to the UN.
The EPLF transforms into the People's Front for Democracy and Justice, the sole ruling party under President Isaias Afwerki.
A territorial dispute with Yemen over the Hanish Islands in the Red Sea flares into armed conflict.
Eritrea ratifies a national constitution, though it is never fully implemented.
A border dispute over Badme erupts into a devastating full-scale war with Ethiopia.
Eritrea and Ethiopia sign the Algiers Agreement ending a war that killed tens of thousands, with a UN peacekeeping buffer zone established.
The government cracks down on dissent, jailing reform-minded officials (the G-15) and shutting down the private press.
An international boundary commission awards the disputed town of Badme to Eritrea, but Ethiopia refuses to implement the ruling.
The UN Security Council imposes sanctions on Eritrea over alleged support for al-Shabaab militants in Somalia.
A UN commission of inquiry accuses the Eritrean government of crimes against humanity, including indefinite national military conscription.
Eritrea and Ethiopia sign a historic peace declaration ending two decades of hostility, reopening borders and restoring relations.
Eritrean troops intervene in Ethiopia's Tigray War, drawing international condemnation for alleged atrocities.
Eritrea remains one of the world's most closed states under continued one-party rule, with no elections held since independence.
